These pillars are essential acts of worship that guide a Muslim's life.

query: How do different religions view the afterlife?
response:
Different religious traditions offer diverse perspectives on the afterlife. In Islam, the Qur'an emphasizes that ["every soul shall taste of death"](https://oceanlibrary.com/quran_rodwell-tr?paraId=para_292) and will be judged, with the righteous entering "gardens with palaces" where they will abide forever. Christianity, as expressed in the *Gospel of John*, promises believers that ["he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-john?paraId=para_425), highlighting resurrection and eternal life. Sikhism, through the *Guru Granth Sahib*, speaks of the soul wandering through incarnations, finding peace only through the divine name.

Buddhism views the ultimate goal as Nirvana, described in the *Dhammapada* as ["the highest happiness"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17245), transcending the cycle of rebirth. Hinduism, in the *Bhagavad Gita*, teaches that the soul is eternal, ["never the spirit was born; the spirit shall cease to be never"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bhagavad-gita?paraId=para_30). The Bahá'í Faith, as articulated by 'Abdu'l-Bahá, likens the body to a cage and the spirit to a bird, suggesting that after death, the spirit continues to exist in a ["paradise of delights"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16714).

In Taoism, life and death are seen as part of a natural cycle, with wisdom recognizing the ["immutable continuity"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18778) of these states. Judaism, as reflected in the *Zohar*, holds that the spirit returns to God, who gave it, when ["the dust returns to the earth"](https://oceanlibrary.com/zohar-volume-1?paraId=para_1161). Each tradition offers a unique lens through which to view the journey beyond this life.

Women*. Buddhism, while not explicitly stating the Golden Rule, embodies it through teachings on compassion and mindfulness, emphasizing the interconnectedness of all beings. These diverse expressions reflect a shared commitment to empathy and mutual respect across these spiritual paths.

query: How do Buddhism and Hinduism differ on the concept of self?
response:
Buddhism and Hinduism offer distinct perspectives on the concept of self. In Buddhism, the idea of self is fundamentally challenged; the Buddha teaches that ["form is not-self, feeling is not-self, perception is not-self"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17256), emphasizing the impermanent and non-essential nature of these aggregates, *Majjhima Nikaya 109 - Mahapunnama Sutta (Greater Full-moon Night)*. This leads to the understanding that attachment to these aggregates results in suffering, as seen in the teaching that clinging to them leads to ["long-term loss & suffering"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19403), *Samyutta Nikaya 22.85 - Yamaka Sutta: To Yamaka*.

In contrast, Hinduism often embraces a more affirmative view of the self. The concept of "Tat Tvam Asi" or ["Thou art That"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19898), *The Tattva-Muktavali (Liberation Through Truth)*, suggests a unity between the individual soul (Atman) and the ultimate reality (Brahman). This phrase is central to the Advaita Vedanta philosophy, which posits that the self is ultimately identical with the divine. Thus, while Buddhism deconstructs the notion of a permanent self, Hinduism often seeks to affirm a deeper, unified self that transcends individual identity.

The fast is described as a means to purify the soul, with Rumi poetically expressing that ["fasting is the key to Solomon's seal"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17096), symbolizing wisdom and spiritual insight.

Judaism also places great importance on fasting, particularly on Yom Kippur, where it is a commandment to ["afflict your souls"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17800) as a form of atonement and repentance. Hindu texts, like the Mahabharata, discuss fasting as a form of penance and spiritual discipline, suggesting that true penance involves ["renunciation of acts and humility"](https://oceanlibrary.com/mahabharata-12_vyasa?paraId=para_1192). In Buddhism, fasting is part of the Uposatha practice, where monks emulate the arahants by ["abstaining from food at night"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18943), focusing on spiritual development. Each tradition highlights fasting as a means to deepen one's spiritual practice and connection to the divine.

query: How is prayer described in different traditions?
response:
Prayer is a profound and varied practice across different religious traditions. In Islam, it is through the ["remembrance of Alláh"](https://oceanlibrary.com/meaning-of-the-glorious-quran_muhammad?paraId=para_1774) that hearts find rest, highlighting the meditative and calming nature of prayer, *The Meaning of the Glorious Qur’án (Pickthall)*. Judaism likens prayer to incense, with the Psalmist expressing, ["Let my prayer be set forth before thee as incense"](https://oceanlibrary.com/book-of-psalms-tehilim_david?paraId=para_2627), *Psalms*, suggesting a fragrant offering rising to the divine. In Sikhism, the practice of meditating on the Name of the Lord is emphasized, as seen in the encouragement to ["meditate on the Name of the Lord, Har, Har"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20186), *Guru Granth Sahib - Raag Asa*.

query: What do the texts say about life after death?

In Islam, the Qur'an emphasizes that ["every soul will taste of death"](https://oceanlibrary.com/meaning-of-the-glorious-quran_muhammad?paraId=para_488) and highlights the Day of Resurrection as a time when deeds are recompensed. Sikh teachings in the *Guru Granth Sahib* describe a process where ["good and bad deeds are added up"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20186) and one's account is settled in the divine court. Christianity, as expressed in the *Gospel of John*, offers the promise that in the afterlife, there are ["many mansions"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-john?paraId=para_529) prepared for believers.

In Taoism, the *Tao Te Ching* speaks of life and death as part of a continuous cycle, where returning to one's root is seen as entering a state of rest and renewal, recognizing the ["immutable continuity"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18778) of existence. Judaism, through texts like the *Mishneh Torah*, envisions the World-to-Come as a place where the righteous delight in the ["radiance of the Divine Presence"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17798), free from earthly needs. The Bahá'í Faith teaches that the soul continues to progress after death, attaining a state that is beyond the changes of this world, as Bahá'u'lláh explains that the soul will ["continue to progress until it attaineth the presence of God"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bahaullah-and-the-new-era_john-esslemont?paraId=para_1082).

query: How do religions explain evil?
response:
Religions offer diverse explanations for the presence of evil. In Sikhism, the concept of Maya, or illusion, is central; it is described as ["fascinating Maya"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20183) that leads people into doubt and attachment, causing them to forget the divine, *Guru Granth Sahib - Anand Sahib*. In the Bahá'í Faith, ‘Abdu’l-Bahá explains that evil is not a real entity but rather ["the absence of good"](https://siftersearch.com/document/15205), akin to darkness being the absence of light, *Star of the West*. Hinduism often attributes the apparent injustices and sufferings to the law of Karma, where one's actions in past lives influence their current circumstances, as seen in the *Mahabharata*.

Judaism discusses the concept of the evil inclination, or ["yetzer hara"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18123), which is an inherent part of human nature, leading individuals away from righteousness, *Aggadat Bereshit*. In Islam, the story of Adam and Iblis illustrates how disobedience and pride can lead to evil actions, with Iblis refusing to prostrate before Adam, thus becoming an enemy to humanity, *Stories of the Prophets*. Christianity, through the writings of Saint Augustine, speaks of the fall of angels and the duality of light and darkness, where evil is associated with pride and self-advancement, *City of God and Christian Doctrine*.

Buddhism identifies ignorance as the root of unskillful qualities, leading to a chain of wrong actions and suffering, as noted in the *Sutra Collection (A, Part-2)*. Finally, Taoism suggests that desires and preferences disrupt one's natural state, causing a loss of virtue and harmony with the Tao, *Chuang Tzu - Mystic, Moralist, and Social Reformer*. Each tradition provides a unique lens through which to understand the complex nature of evil and its role in human life.

query: What is the relationship between faith and reason?
response:
The relationship between faith and reason is a rich tapestry woven across various religious traditions. In the Bahá'í Faith, 'Abdu'l-Bahá emphasizes that ["religion and science are the two wings upon which man's intelligence can soar"](https://oceanlibrary.com/paris-talks_abdul-baha), suggesting that both are essential for true understanding. In Christianity, Paul writes that ["the foolishness of God is wiser than men"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-first-epistlethe-corinthians?paraId=para_19), highlighting the paradoxical wisdom found in divine faith. The Jewish tradition echoes this sentiment, stating that ["the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-book-of-proverbs?paraId=para_10), suggesting that reverence and faith are foundational to true knowledge.

The Taoist perspective, as expressed in the *Tao Te Ching*, advises to ["abandon sageliness and discard wisdom"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18799), implying that true understanding transcends conventional wisdom. Buddhism, on the other hand, encourages a practical approach, advising the Kalamas to rely on teachings that are ["immediately verifiable and capable of laying a firm foundation"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-hijk?paraId=para_1106) for moral discipline.

In Sikhism, the *Guru Granth Sahib* teaches that within the mind are ["gems, jewels and rubies"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20185), accessible through the Guru's teachings, which blend faith with inner wisdom. Islam warns against limiting the divine through human description, stating that ["whosoever tries to limit the Creator by description or comparison hath verily disbelieved"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bahaullah-as-fulfilment-of-theophanic-promise_khazeh-fananapazir?paraId=para_73), emphasizing the transcendence of God beyond human reason.

Each tradition offers a unique perspective, yet they collectively suggest that faith and reason are not opposing forces but complementary paths to deeper understanding.

response: Progressive revelation is the concept that divine guidance is revealed progressively over time through a series of messengers, each bringing teachings suited to the needs of their era. In the Bahá'í Faith, this is articulated as the ["fulfillment of its predecessors"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bahaullah-and-the-new-era_john-esslemont?paraId=para_719), with each revelation building upon the last, like a bud, flower, and fruit. The Qur'án supports this idea, acknowledging that ["messengers indeed have been denied before thee"](https://oceanlibrary.com/meaning-of-the-glorious-quran_muhammad?paraId=para_842), indicating a continuity of divine messages. In Christianity, the concept of a ["new covenant"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-epistle-to-hebrews?paraId=para_109) reflects the evolution of divine law through Jesus. Hinduism also presents a form of progressive revelation through the concept of ["avatárs"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17369), where Vishṇu descends in various forms to guide humanity. Sikhism recognizes the continuity of divine guidance through the succession of Gurus, as seen in the Guru Granth Sahib. Judaism speaks of a future covenant where God's law will be written ["in their hearts"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-book-of-jeremiah?paraId=para_670), signifying an evolving relationship with the divine. The Taoist texts emphasize understanding the Great Tâo, which involves a progression from understanding Heaven to the application of virtues. Each tradition, in its own way, reflects the idea that spiritual truth unfolds progressively to meet the evolving needs of humanity.
